name: Almagro to Yacimiento de Ore
name_original: Almagro a Yacimiento de Oreto y Zuqueca – circular por Valenzuela de Calatrava
description: This moderate 30.9-mile mountain biking route explores the historical Oreto and Zuqueca Archaeological Site and the Jabalón River valley.
region_intro: Mountain biking around Almagro offers diverse terrain, from the unique volcanic zone near Cerro de la Yezosa to the small Paleozoic mountain ranges featuring shallow creeks. The region provides access to extensive plains and significant wetlands, including the Tablas de Daimiel National Park. This varied topography creates a range of mountain bike trails suitable for different skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Almagro The most popular mountain bike trail is Almagro to Yacimiento de Ore, a 30.9 miles (49.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 51 minutes to complete, leading riders through the regional landscape towards an archaeological site. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is The jars – Laguna del Prado loop from Almagro, a moderate 31.5 miles (50.6 km) path that explores the unique wetland ecosystems surrounding Laguna del Prado. Local mountain bikers also love the Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro) – The jars loop from Almagro, a 23.0 miles (37.0 km) trail leading through historical sites and natural features, often completed in about 2 hours 30 minutes. Mountain biking around Almagro is defined by volcanic features, small Paleozoic mountain ranges, and extensive plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to longer routes exploring natural parks. The routes in Almagro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 25 reviews. More than 250 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Almagro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of mountain bike trails around Almagro?
A: The mountain bike trails around Almagro offer a range of difficulties, with the majority being &lt;strong&gt;moderate&lt;/strong&gt;. Out of nearly 60 available tours, 51 are classified as moderate, providing a good balance for most riders. There are also a few easier and more challenging options for different skill levels.

Q: When is the best time of year for mountain biking in Almagro?
A: Almagro experiences a sub-desert Mediterranean climate with strong sunshine and high temperatures, especially in summer. Therefore, the best times for mountain biking are typically during the &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn months&lt;/strong&gt; when temperatures are milder. Early mornings or late afternoons are also advisable during warmer periods to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Are there family-friendly mountain bike trails in Almagro?
A: Yes, Almagro offers some mountain bike trails suitable for families. While most routes are moderate, there are 6 trails classified as &lt;strong&gt;easy&lt;/strong&gt;. These routes often feature gentler gradients and less technical terrain, making them more accessible for riders of varying ages and abilities. Always check the specific trail details for suitability before heading out.

Q: What natural landmarks or points of interest can I see while mountain biking in Almagro?
A: The region around Almagro is rich in natural features. You can explore a unique volcanic zone, small Paleozoic mountain ranges, and significant wetlands. Notable points of interest include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Prado&lt;/a&gt;, a unique wetland ecosystem, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2713621" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vega del Jabalón Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The area also provides access to the Tablas de Daimiel National Park and Lagunas de Ruidera Natural Park, known for their unique ecosystems.

Q: Are there many loop mountain bike trails available in Almagro?
A: Yes, many mountain bike routes in Almagro are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31868628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The jars – Laguna del Prado loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, which explores wetland ecosystems, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31845983" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro) – The jars loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, which passes historical sites.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the mountain bike trails around Almagro?
A: The diverse landscapes around Almagro, particularly near the Tablas de Daimiel National Park and Cabañeros National Park, are home to various wildlife. You might spot diverse birdlife in the wetlands, and Cabañeros is known for its deer herds and imperial eagles. While riding, keep an eye out for local fauna, especially in quieter, less disturbed areas.

Q: Can I bring my dog on mountain bike trails in Almagro?
A: While many outdoor areas in Spain are dog-friendly, it's always best to check local regulations for specific trails or natural parks. If dogs are permitted, ensure they are well-behaved, kept on a leash where required, and that you carry bags to clean up after them. Consider the climate, as high temperatures can be challenging for dogs.

Q: Are there any specific mountain bike parks or dedicated trails in Almagro?
A: Yes, Almagro and its surroundings feature dedicated areas for mountain biking. You can find several bike parks and specific MTB trails, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5440353" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Atalaya MTB Trails&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7718535" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;MTB Trails Golf-Atalaya&lt;/a&gt;. These areas often provide varied terrain and features specifically designed for mountain bikers.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about mountain biking in Almagro?
A: The mountain biking experience in Almagro is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 25 reviews. Riders often praise the &lt;strong&gt;varied terrain&lt;/strong&gt;, which includes volcanic features, small mountain ranges, and extensive plains, offering diverse challenges and scenic views. The well-maintained network of trails and the opportunity to explore unique natural parks are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural points of interest accessible by mountain bike?
A: Yes, the region integrates historical elements into its natural landscape. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130708" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro) – Molinos de Moral loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt; leads through areas with historical significance. The Sierra del Alto de Almagro, though a bit further, also features sites like the old Tres Pacos Mine, hinting at the area's rich past, which can be explored on more challenging rides.

Q: What are some of the longer mountain bike routes available in Almagro?
A: For those seeking longer rides, Almagro offers several routes over 45 km. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026555753/almagro-to-yacimiento-de-ore" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almagro to Yacimiento de Ore&lt;/a&gt; trail is nearly 50 km long, leading through regional landscapes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129147" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro) – Virgen de los Santos loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 51 km.

Q: Are there opportunities for mountain biking near lakes or reservoirs in Almagro?
A: Absolutely. The region around Almagro features several beautiful lakes and reservoirs that can be incorporated into your mountain biking adventures. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31868628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The jars – Laguna del Prado loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt; specifically explores the unique wetland ecosystems surrounding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Prado&lt;/a&gt;. You can also find routes near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2713621" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vega del Jabalón Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, offering scenic waterside riding.
tips:
The archaeological importance of the environment in which the site is located has been well known since ancient times, since the Cerro de Oreto is located nearby, which contains the ruins of the capital of Oretania, Oretum according to sources, (a Roman province that included from north to south beyond Sierra Morena and extended to the end of the Bética); the Cerro de los Obispos, an important Iberian site on whose slopes appeared some burials attributed to Visigothic bishops (one of them Bishop Amador, as recorded on the tombstone that covered his tomb); several late-Roman necropolises corresponding to that classical city and the Roman bridge over the Jabalón ordered to be built by Publio Baebio Venusto, dated between the end of the 1st century and the beginning of the 2nd century AD, and which has currently been covered by the waters of the Reservoir.

Numerous pieces belonging to different periods have been found, among which are coins, ceramics, farm elements, and loom stones.

The Parish Church of San Bartolomé dates, according to the Relations of Felipe II, from 1560, when the first inhabitants of the town built it on the site of the old Arab castle, and using reused materials, many of the stones of its walls can be seen Visigoth and medieval crosses and motifs. It is a church with a basilica plan, with an extension towards the apse, a gabled roof, and without a tower, topped by a belfry with a bell tower.

Destroyed in the 1936 war, it also housed the Brotherhood of Santiago, since the disappearance of its hermitage in the 19th century. On July 23, 1983, the new hermitage was inaugurated on the ruins of the old one, highlighting the porch treated with white concrete set in flexible formwork.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Almagro a Yacimiento de Oreto y Zuqueca mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: This moderate mountain biking tour is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 51 minutes to complete. This duration can vary based on your pace, stops for sightseeing, and fitness level.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: You can expect varied terrain on this route. While specific details are limited, similar trails in the area feature a mix of surfaces including dirt tracks, gravel paths, paved sections, and some 'off-grid' parts. It's designed for mountain biking, so be prepared for diverse ground conditions.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and historical sites can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a rich blend of history and natural beauty. You'll pass by the significant Oreto and Zuqueca Archaeological Site, which showcases remains from Iberian, Roman, Visigothic, and Arab periods. The trail also winds through the unique volcanic landscape of the Campo de Calatrava region, with views of the Jabalón River valley and its reservoir, and traditional Spanish towns like Almagro and Valenzuela de Calatrava. You might also spot the Parish of Saint Bartholomew the Apostle and the Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro).

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this route?
faqs.answer: Given the climate in the Campo de Calatrava region, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally the most pleasant seasons for mountain biking. Temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery is often at its best. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be chilly, so plan accordingly.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this route?
faqs.answer: The route is circular and connects Almagro and Valenzuela de Calatrava. You can typically start and find parking in either town. Almagro, being a larger town, likely offers more designated parking options, making it a convenient starting point for your ride.

faqs.question: Is this mountain biking route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'moderate' difficulty. While it doesn't explicitly detail technical features, its length (nearly 50 km) and moderate elevation changes suggest it requires a reasonable level of fitness and basic mountain biking skills. Beginners with some experience might find it challenging but rewarding, while absolute novices might prefer shorter, flatter routes first.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Ruta de los Navajos y el Volcán de la Estrella, the Ruta de Don Quijote, the Senda del LLano, and the Cañada de Añavete, among others. This offers a chance to experience different local trails.

faqs.question: Is the Almagro a Yacimiento de Oreto y Zuqueca – circular por Valenzuela de Calatrava route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are welcome on trails in rural Spain, especially if kept under control. However, when passing through towns or near archaeological sites like Oreto y Zuqueca, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash. Please ensure you clean up after your pet to maintain the cleanliness of the area.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to cycle this route, as it primarily uses public paths and roads. The archaeological site of Oreto y Zuqueca may have its own visiting hours or entry requirements if you plan to explore it on foot, but the trail itself is freely accessible.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, since the route connects the towns of Almagro and Valenzuela de Calatrava, you'll find various options for food and refreshments in both locations. Almagro, in particular, is known for its charming squares and eateries where you can refuel before or after your ride.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this mountain biking tour?
faqs.answer: For this moderate mountain biking tour, it's advisable to pack essentials like sufficient water, snacks, a basic bike repair kit (including a spare tube and pump), sun protection (sunscreen, hat, sunglasses), and appropriate cycling attire. A first-aid kit and a fully charged phone are also recommended for safety.

waypoints.name: Parish of Saint Bartholomew the Apostle
